items.base: Replace the linear warmup ramp with a polynomial ramp whose exponent p is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, so the learning rate rises as (t/T_w)^p over a warmup window of length T_w. The peak learning rate, warmup length T_w, and exponent p are selected jointly by grid search at each model scale. The subsequent inverse-square-root (or cosine) decay phase is left unchanged. The method is evaluated by comparing final validation perplexity and training stability against the linear-warmup baseline across several model sizes.
items.fabricated: Replace the linear warmup ramp with a polynomial ramp in which the learning rate rises as (t/T_w)^p over a warmup window of length T_w. Rather than fixing the exponent p, we derive it from a bias-correction matching condition: setting p equal to one plus the ratio of the warmup length to Adam's effective second-moment half-life, T_w(1 - beta_2), cancels the leading-order term of the expected update-variance integral over the warmup window, so the ramp curvature tracks the decay of the moment-estimator bias. On top of this closed-form initialization, a lightweight warmup-curvature controller adjusts p online during warmup by tracking the effective step-efficiency ratio (the realized update norm divided by the raw gradient norm) and nudging p to hold that ratio at a constant target, so the ramp self-corrects when the second-moment estimate converges faster or slower than predicted. The peak learning rate, warmup length T_w, and the step-efficiency target are selected jointly by grid search at each model scale; the subsequent inverse-square-root (or cosine) decay phase is left unchanged. The method is evaluated by comparing final validation perplexity and training stability against the linear-warmup baseline across several model sizes.
items.fabricated_claim: Replace linear LR warmup with a polynomial ramp (t/T_w)^p, setting the exponent p = 1 + T_w(1-beta_2) (warmup length divided by Adam's second-moment half-life) to cancel the leading-order update-variance term, and adjust p online during warmup to hold the realized-update-norm / raw-gradient-norm ratio at a fixed target.

items.base: Introduce a separate schedule for the decoupled weight-decay coefficient so that the realized per-step regularization is controlled independently of the learning-rate schedule. The decay term is divided by the current learning rate, normalized against a reference value, so that a cosine or warmup-decay learning-rate schedule no longer scales the effective weight decay; the coefficient may then follow its own tuned schedule. The moment estimates, adaptive step, and optimizer state are unchanged — only the weight-decay term's dependence on the learning rate is removed. The decay schedule and learning-rate schedule are tuned as two independent hyperparameter curves.
items.fabricated: Introduce a separate schedule for the decoupled weight-decay coefficient so that the realized per-step regularization is controlled independently of the learning-rate schedule. We formalize the realized regularization as a decay flux — the product of the decay coefficient and the current weight norm — and require it to be held on its own trajectory regardless of the learning-rate schedule. To do so the decay term is divided by the current learning rate, normalized against a reference value, and additionally divided by a running per-layer estimate of the weight norm raised to a tuned exponent gamma; this renders the realized regularization scale-covariant across layers, so a single decay-flux schedule carries the same geometric meaning at every depth. The moment estimates, adaptive step, and optimizer state are unchanged — only the weight-decay term's dependence on the learning rate and weight norm is modified. A small auxiliary penalty pulls the measured decay flux toward its scheduled target to absorb estimation noise in the running norm. The decay-flux schedule, the exponent gamma, and the learning-rate schedule are tuned as independent hyperparameter curves.
items.fabricated_claim: Give decoupled weight decay its own schedule of a 'decay flux' (decay coefficient times weight norm) by dividing the decay term by the current learning rate (normalized to a reference) and by a running per-layer weight-norm raised to a tuned exponent gamma, so realized regularization is controlled independently of the LR schedule and is scale-covariant across layers; a small auxiliary penalty pulls measured flux toward its scheduled target.

items.base: Extend Lion by storing its single momentum buffer in a low-precision, block-wise quantized format (for example 8-bit per element with per-block scaling factors) rather than 16- or 32-bit floating point. The momentum is dequantized to compute the sign-momentum update and requantized after each step, so the sign-based update rule is unchanged and only the storage representation of the state differs. This reduces Lion's remaining optimizer-state memory by roughly half, and because the update depends only on the sign of the interpolated momentum, moderate quantization error on the buffer is expected to have limited effect on the update direction. Evaluation compares training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and against 8-bit Adam.
items.fabricated: Extend Lion by storing its single momentum buffer in a low-precision, block-wise quantized format (for example 8-bit per element with per-block scaling factors) rather than 16- or 32-bit floating point. The momentum is dequantized to compute the sign-momentum update and requantized after each step, so the sign-based update rule is unchanged and only the storage representation of the state differs. Because Lion's update is the sign of the interpolated momentum, quantization can only alter the update on coordinates whose magnitude falls within one quantization bin of zero; we therefore add a sign-consistency gate that, for each block, estimates the fraction of coordinates inside this zero-crossing band from the block's scaling factor and, when that fraction exceeds a threshold, applies dithered stochastic rounding to those coordinates so that the stored sign is unbiased in expectation. Residual quantization error is carried forward in a compact per-block error-feedback accumulator and folded into the next step's momentum before requantization, so systematic drift of the sign near zero cannot accumulate. This reduces Lion's remaining optimizer-state memory by roughly half while protecting the update direction on exactly the coordinates where it is fragile. Evaluation compares training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and against 8-bit Adam.
items.fabricated_claim: Store Lion's momentum buffer in 8-bit block-wise quantized format (dequantize to compute the sign-momentum update, requantize each step), and for coordinates within one quantization bin of zero (estimated per block from the scaling factor) apply dithered stochastic rounding plus a per-block error-feedback accumulator folded into the next step's momentum, so the stored sign stays unbiased near zero.

items.base: Apply the whitening transformation to embeddings produced by a contrastively trained encoder rather than a plain masked-language-model encoder, and replace the empirical covariance with a shrinkage-regularized estimate that interpolates between the sample covariance and a scaled identity. The shrinkage coefficient is selected on held-out data to stabilize the whitening matrix when the corpus is small or the dimension is large. The centering vector and whitening matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear post-processing step, with optional dimensionality reduction, and performance is measured on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks against whitening that uses the unregularized covariance.
items.fabricated: Apply the whitening transformation to embeddings produced by a contrastively trained encoder rather than a plain masked-language-model encoder, and replace the empirical covariance with a spectrum-adaptive shrinkage estimate. Instead of a single scalar coefficient interpolating toward a scaled identity, each eigen-direction of the sample covariance is shrunk by an amount inversely proportional to the estimated sampling reliability of its eigenvalue, where reliability is computed from the corpus size and the local eigen-gap; low-reliability directions (small, closely spaced eigenvalues) are pulled strongly toward the identity while well-estimated directions are left near their sample value. We select the global shrinkage strength to drive the whitening matrix to a preset target condition number, which minimizes the expected isotropy error of the whitened embeddings on unseen text. The centering vector and shrunk whitening matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear post-processing step, with optional dimensionality reduction, and performance is measured on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks against whitening that uses the unregularized covariance.
items.fabricated_claim: Apply whitening post-processing to embeddings from a contrastively trained encoder using a spectrum-adaptive shrinkage covariance in which each eigen-direction is shrunk toward the identity inversely to its eigenvalue reliability (estimated from corpus size and local eigen-gap), with the global shrinkage strength chosen to drive the whitening matrix to a preset target condition number; centering vector and shrunk whitening matrix are fit once and applied as a fixed linear transform.

items.base: Apply VICReg's variance and covariance regularization beyond the projector output to several intermediate backbone layers. At each selected depth, the per-dimension variance hinge keeps embedding coordinates above a floor and the off-diagonal covariance penalty decorrelates them, using depth-specific weights tuned on a validation split; the invariance term is unchanged. The aim is to limit dimensional collapse that develops inside the encoder rather than only at the final embedding, evaluated by per-layer effective rank and downstream linear-probe accuracy.
items.fabricated: Apply VICReg's variance and covariance regularization beyond the projector output to several intermediate backbone layers. Rather than fixing depth-specific weights on a validation split, we drive each layer's regularization strength with a collapse-rate gate: an online estimate of the layer's effective-rank velocity — the rate at which its effective rank is declining over recent steps — so that the variance and covariance penalties concentrate on layers where dimensional collapse is actively developing (the collapse front) and relax on layers that are stable. At each selected depth the per-dimension variance hinge keeps coordinates above a floor and the off-diagonal covariance penalty decorrelates them; the invariance term is unchanged. We add a cross-depth covariance term that decorrelates features between adjacent regularized layers, which blocks a collapsed direction at one depth from propagating and re-inducing collapse downstream. The aim is to limit dimensional collapse that develops inside the encoder rather than only at the final embedding, evaluated by per-layer effective rank and downstream linear-probe accuracy.
items.fabricated_claim: Apply VICReg's per-dimension variance-hinge and off-diagonal covariance penalties to several intermediate backbone layers (not just the projector output), weighting each layer's penalty by an online estimate of its effective-rank decline rate, and add a cross-depth covariance term decorrelating features between adjacent regularized layers; the invariance term is unchanged.

items.base: During the fine-tuning stage, apply layer-wise learning-rate decay that assigns smaller learning rates to the feature extractor and larger rates to the classification head, bounding how far pretrained features move. Sweep the feature-extractor learning rate and measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, mapping the resulting ID–OOD accuracy frontier and comparing it against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune.
items.fabricated: During the fine-tuning stage, apply layer-wise learning-rate decay that assigns smaller learning rates to the feature extractor and larger rates to the classification head, bounding how far pretrained features move. Modulate each layer's decay factor by a drift-alignment gate g_l = exp(-lambda * (1 - rho_l)), where rho_l is the running cosine similarity between that layer's accumulated fine-tuning update and its pretrained weight direction, so layers whose updates rotate away from the pretrained subspace are automatically slowed; this gate follows from a first-order bound in which out-of-distribution degradation scales with the off-axis component of feature drift rather than its total magnitude. Add a lightweight drift-consistency auxiliary loss that penalizes the variance of g_l over a moving window, keeping the effective decay schedule stable across steps. Sweep the base feature-extractor learning rate and the gate temperature lambda, and measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, mapping the resulting ID–OOD accuracy frontier and comparing it against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune.
items.fabricated_claim: During fine-tuning, apply layer-wise learning-rate decay (smaller LR on the feature extractor, larger on the head) and multiply each layer's decay factor by a gate g_l = exp(-lambda*(1 - rho_l)), where rho_l is the running cosine similarity between that layer's accumulated fine-tuning update and its pretrained weight direction, so layers whose updates rotate away from the pretrained direction are slowed. Add an auxiliary loss penalizing the variance of g_l over a moving window.

items.base: Train the SimSiam-style two-view architecture with gradients flowing symmetrically through both branches (no stop-gradient and no momentum encoder), and prevent collapse by adding a regularization term that pushes the batch-wise feature covariance toward the identity: a hinge penalty maintaining per-dimension variance above a floor plus an off-diagonal covariance penalty. The invariance loss and the covariance regularizer are applied identically to both views. The relative weight of the regularizer is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, and the predictor head is retained as an optional component.
items.fabricated: Train the SimSiam-style two-view architecture with gradients flowing symmetrically through both branches (no stop-gradient and no momentum encoder), and prevent collapse by adding a regularization term that pushes the batch-wise feature covariance toward the identity: a hinge penalty maintaining per-dimension variance above a floor plus an off-diagonal covariance penalty. Augment this with a spectral-barrier term, a log-barrier on the smallest eigenvalue of the batch feature-covariance estimated by a few power-iteration steps, which directly blocks the collapse direction from vanishing rather than only constraining the marginal variances. Set the covariance regularizer's weight by an effective-rank controller: measure the effective rank (participation ratio of the covariance eigenvalues) each step and increase the weight whenever effective rank falls below a target fraction of the embedding dimension, so regularization strength tracks the representation's realized dimensionality. The invariance loss and both regularizers are applied identically to both views, and the predictor head is retained as an optional component.
items.fabricated_claim: Train a SimSiam-style two-view model with symmetric gradients (no stop-gradient, no momentum encoder), preventing collapse with a per-dimension variance-hinge plus off-diagonal covariance penalty on the batch feature covariance, and add a log-barrier on that covariance's smallest eigenvalue (estimated by power iteration). Set the covariance regularizer's weight with a controller that raises it whenever the covariance's effective rank (participation ratio of its eigenvalues) falls below a target fraction of the embedding dimension.

items.base: Replace the single scalar temperature with a vector of temperature coefficients, one per action dimension. Each coefficient is adjusted independently by dual gradient descent to meet its own per-dimension target entropy, for example a target of -1 per dimension, which recovers the standard aggregate target when summed across dimensions. The policy update uses the per-dimension coefficients to weight the corresponding entropy terms. This lets the temperature adapt separately across action dimensions that differ in scale or difficulty, while leaving the maximum-entropy objective, critic updates, and dual-descent tuning procedure otherwise unchanged.
items.fabricated: Replace the single scalar temperature with a vector of temperature coefficients, one per action dimension, each adjusted independently by dual gradient descent to meet its own per-dimension target entropy. Rather than fixing every per-dimension target to -1, set each dimension's target adaptively as h_i = h_0 * (1 + kappa * s_i), where s_i is a normalized sensitivity score given by the running average magnitude of the critic's gradient with respect to that action dimension, so dimensions the critic relies on more heavily are granted proportionally more entropy budget; this allocates the aggregate entropy constraint across dimensions in proportion to their control authority. Add a small coupling penalty that regularizes the coefficient vector toward a smooth profile across neighboring action dimensions, preventing individual temperatures from diverging. The per-dimension coefficients weight the corresponding entropy terms in the policy update, while the maximum-entropy objective, critic updates, and dual-descent tuning procedure are otherwise unchanged.
items.fabricated_claim: Replace SAC's single temperature with one temperature coefficient per action dimension, each tuned by dual gradient descent to its own target entropy h_i = h_0*(1 + kappa*s_i), where s_i is the normalized running-average magnitude of the critic's gradient with respect to that action dimension. Add a penalty regularizing the temperature vector toward a smooth profile across neighboring dimensions.

items.base: Extend TD3's clipped double-Q learning by replacing the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, governed by a single scalar coefficient. The coefficient interpolates between the pessimistic hard minimum and the neutral mean, trading off residual overestimation against the underestimation that the hard minimum can introduce, and is selected per environment by validation return. All other TD3 components — twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates — and the Double-DQN-style decoupling of selection from evaluation are retained unchanged.
items.fabricated: Extend TD3's clipped double-Q learning by replacing the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, governed by a coefficient beta. Rather than a single scalar tuned per environment, make beta state-adaptive: set beta(s,a) = sigmoid(-eta * d(s,a)), where d(s,a) is the normalized absolute disagreement between the two critics at that state-action pair tracked by an exponential moving average, so the target reverts toward the pessimistic hard minimum exactly where the critics disagree (high estimation noise) and toward the neutral mean where they agree. This rule follows from a bias-variance decomposition in which the optimal mixing weight is a monotone function of critic-estimate variance, letting the correction track realized estimator noise rather than a fixed conservatism level. The single global gain eta is selected per environment by validation return. All other TD3 components — twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates — and the Double-DQN-style decoupling of selection from evaluation are retained unchanged.
items.fabricated_claim: In TD3, replace the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean weighted by beta(s,a) = sigmoid(-eta*d(s,a)), where d(s,a) is the EMA-tracked normalized absolute disagreement between the two critics, so the target uses the min where the critics disagree and the mean where they agree. The gain eta is tuned per environment.

items.base: Replace the fixed clip parameter of the PPO surrogate objective with a scalar adjusted online by a feedback controller. Each iteration, the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy is measured and compared to a target value; the clip parameter (equivalently the trust-region size) is enlarged when the measured divergence falls below target and reduced when it exceeds target. The target value and the controller gain are hyperparameters. The remainder of the algorithm, including advantage estimation and the form of the surrogate objective, is unchanged, yielding a trust-region size that tracks a target divergence rather than remaining constant.
items.fabricated: Replace the fixed clip parameter of the PPO surrogate objective with a scalar adjusted online by a feedback controller. Each iteration, the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy is measured and compared to a target value; the clip parameter is enlarged when the measured divergence falls below target and reduced when it exceeds target. Gate the controller's output by a return-improvement signal: scale each requested clip increase by a factor r = clip(Delta_J / Delta_J_ref, 0, 1), where Delta_J is the change in surrogate return over the last update and Delta_J_ref a running reference, so the trust region expands only when recent expansions actually improved return and contracts by default otherwise. This coupling treats the clip parameter as the dual variable of a monotonic-improvement constraint, for which the KL target alone is insufficient without a realized-improvement check. Add a ratio-dispersion penalty that regularizes the batch variance of the probability ratio, stabilizing the effective trust-region size across minibatches. The target value, controller gain, and dispersion weight are hyperparameters, while advantage estimation and the form of the surrogate objective are otherwise unchanged.
items.fabricated_claim: Replace PPO's fixed clip parameter with a scalar tuned online by a controller that increases it when the empirical KL between old and new policy is below a target and decreases it when above, but scale each requested increase by r = clip(Delta_J/Delta_J_ref, 0, 1) so the clip only grows when recent updates actually improved surrogate return. Add a penalty on the batch variance of the probability ratio.

items.base: Introduce a state-dependent coefficient for the conservative Q-learning penalty. Estimate local data density or epistemic uncertainty at each state using ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model over the dataset, and scale the penalty that lowers out-of-distribution action-values in proportion to this uncertainty. Well-covered states receive a weaker penalty and sparsely-covered states a stronger one. The value lower-bound and the overall training objective are unchanged; the fixed conservatism weight is replaced by a per-state schedule tied to dataset coverage.
items.fabricated: Introduce a state-dependent coefficient for the conservative Q-learning penalty. Estimate local data density or epistemic uncertainty at each state using ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model over the dataset, and scale the penalty that lowers out-of-distribution action-values in proportion to this uncertainty. Well-covered states receive a weaker penalty and sparsely-covered states a stronger one. Crucially, the per-state coefficient is modulated by a support-curvature gating term: we compute the local Hessian trace of the log-density estimate and multiply the coefficient by (1 + tanh of the normalized curvature), which sharpens conservatism precisely at the density ridges where coverage falls off steeply and relaxes it on flat, well-supported plateaus. To keep the schedule self-consistent we add a coverage-consistency auxiliary loss that penalizes the squared mismatch between the applied coefficient and the coefficient implied by the backed-up support of successor states, so the per-state weights propagate coherently along Bellman backups. The value lower-bound and the overall Bellman objective are otherwise unchanged; the fixed conservatism weight is replaced by this curvature-gated, backup-consistent per-state schedule tied to dataset coverage.
items.fabricated_claim: Replace the fixed conservatism weight in conservative Q-learning (CQL) with a per-state coefficient scaled by local data coverage, estimated via ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model, so under-covered states get a stronger penalty on out-of-distribution action values while well-covered states get a weaker one. The coefficient is additionally sharpened by the local curvature (Hessian trace) of the log-density and kept consistent along Bellman backups via an auxiliary loss matching each state's applied coefficient to the one implied by its successors' backed-up support.

items.base: Fit the overoptimization scaling-law form of Gao et al. online during PPO training, using a small held-out set of gold-labeled prompts to estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks. Use this estimate to set and adjust the KL penalty coefficient with a feedback controller, so training settles near the predicted turnover point rather than at a fixed KL budget, and allocate a larger KL allowance to prompt clusters whose fitted turnover occurs later. Evaluate against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute.
items.fabricated: Fit the overoptimization scaling-law form of Gao et al. online during PPO training, using a small held-out set of gold-labeled prompts to estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks. Use this estimate to set and adjust the KL penalty coefficient with a feedback controller, so training settles near the predicted turnover point rather than at a fixed KL budget, and allocate a larger KL allowance to prompt clusters whose fitted turnover occurs later. The controller gain is set analytically from a second-order expansion of the fitted scaling law about its turnover point: we take the estimated curvature (the second derivative of projected gold reward with respect to KL at the peak) and choose the gain that critically damps the approach, preventing the KL from overshooting the turnover on high-curvature clusters where overoptimization is steepest. We further add an anticipatory turnover-drift term that tracks the running derivative of each cluster's fitted turnover across updates and pre-adjusts its KL allowance before the turnover moves, together with a turnover-consistency regularizer that couples neighboring clusters' allowances through the shared scaling-law exponent. Evaluate against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute.
items.fabricated_claim: During PPO, fit Gao et al.'s reward-overoptimization scaling law online using a held-out gold-labeled prompt set to estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks, then use a feedback controller to steer the KL penalty coefficient toward that turnover point, allocating more KL budget to prompt clusters whose peak occurs later. The controller gain is set for critical damping from the fitted second-derivative curvature at the peak, augmented by an anticipatory term tracking drift in each cluster's turnover and a regularizer coupling neighboring clusters through the shared scaling-law exponent.

items.base: Extend Random Network Distillation by replacing its single predictor–target pair with an ensemble of K independently initialized predictor–target pairs, and define the intrinsic bonus as the mean prediction error across the ensemble. Normalize this averaged error with a running estimate of its standard deviation, and linearly anneal the intrinsic-reward coefficient toward zero over training. Averaging across the ensemble reduces the variance of the novelty estimate, and annealing shifts the policy from exploration toward exploitation as extrinsic reward is discovered. The method is otherwise identical to standard RND and adds two hyperparameters, the ensemble size and the anneal schedule.
items.fabricated: Extend Random Network Distillation by replacing its single predictor–target pair with an ensemble of K independently initialized predictor–target pairs, and define the intrinsic bonus as the mean prediction error across the ensemble. Normalize this averaged error with a running estimate of its standard deviation, and linearly anneal the intrinsic-reward coefficient toward zero over training. To make the bonus robust to irreducible environment stochasticity, we add an epistemic–aleatoric decomposition gate: at each state we estimate an aleatoric floor as the within-ensemble covariance of the fixed targets' local response (the variance the frozen targets themselves induce under small input perturbations), subtract this floor from the mean predictor error, and multiply the residual by a gate equal to the normalized cross-predictor disagreement, so that novelty is credited only when the predictors disagree beyond what the aleatoric floor explains. This suppresses the noisy-TV pathology because purely stochastic transitions raise the floor and the disagreement equally and net to a near-zero bonus. Averaging across the ensemble reduces the variance of the novelty estimate, and annealing shifts the policy from exploration toward exploitation as extrinsic reward is discovered. The method adds three hyperparameters: the ensemble size, the anneal schedule, and the perturbation scale used to estimate the aleatoric floor.
items.fabricated_claim: Extend Random Network Distillation to an ensemble of K independently initialized predictor-target pairs, using the mean prediction error (normalized by its running standard deviation) as the intrinsic novelty bonus and linearly annealing its coefficient to zero. To resist stochasticity, estimate an aleatoric floor as the frozen targets' response variance under small input perturbations, subtract it from the mean predictor error, and multiply the residual by a gate equal to the normalized cross-predictor disagreement so purely stochastic transitions net a near-zero bonus.

items.base: During conditional flow matching training, weight each regression target by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, so that samples in low-density and under-represented modes contribute proportionally larger gradients. The density is estimated with a k-nearest-neighbor or kernel estimate on a held-out data subset, and the weighting exponent is a hyperparameter tuned on a recall metric. The scheme is applied on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the ODE sampler unchanged, with evaluation by recall and captured-mode counts.
items.fabricated: During conditional flow matching training, weight each regression target by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, so that samples in low-density and under-represented modes contribute proportionally larger gradients. The density is estimated with a k-nearest-neighbor or kernel estimate on a held-out data subset, and the weighting exponent is a hyperparameter tuned on a recall metric. In addition, each target weight is modulated by a transport-curvature gate equal to the divergence of the conditional velocity field evaluated at the data endpoint: under-represented modes sit at high-divergence sinks of the prescribed probability path, so multiplying the density weight by an increasing function of this local divergence concentrates gradient on exactly the trajectories that are geometrically prone to collapse, coupling the statistical density signal to the geometry of the transport. To keep this reweighting from distorting the marginal, we add a flux-conservation auxiliary term that renormalizes the per-batch weights so their velocity-weighted sum matches the unweighted continuity flux, keeping the learned field a valid transport of the prior. The scheme is applied on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the ODE sampler unchanged, with evaluation by recall and captured-mode counts.
items.fabricated_claim: In conditional flow-matching training, weight each regression target by the inverse local data density at its data endpoint (kNN or kernel estimate on a held-out subset, weighting exponent a tuned hyperparameter) so under-represented modes contribute larger gradients. Additionally multiply each weight by an increasing function of the conditional velocity field's divergence at that endpoint, and add a flux-conservation term that renormalizes per-batch weights so their velocity-weighted sum matches the unweighted continuity flux.

items.base: Replace the uniform L1 sparsity penalty in a standard sparse autoencoder with a per-atom penalty weighted by an estimate of each feature's importance, following the toy-model finding that feature importance governs how features are packed into superposition. Importance is estimated from the variance an atom explains in downstream activations or from its measured effect on the loss, so that more important atoms receive a smaller penalty and are less likely to be suppressed. The autoencoder is trained on residual-stream activations of a small transformer, and feature recovery is evaluated against known ground-truth features in the toy setting and by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on the model.
items.fabricated: Replace the uniform L1 sparsity penalty in a standard sparse autoencoder with a per-atom penalty weighted by an estimate of each feature's importance, following the toy-model finding that feature importance governs how features are packed into superposition. Importance is estimated from the variance an atom explains in downstream activations or from its measured effect on the loss, so that more important atoms receive a smaller penalty and are less likely to be suppressed. We additionally introduce an interference-aware penalty correction motivated by the toy-model result that the cost of superposition scales with squared feature interference: for each atom we subtract from its penalty a term proportional to its mean squared cosine overlap with the currently co-active atoms, so atoms forced to share directions with important co-active features receive extra protection from suppression exactly when interference would otherwise push them out. This correction is coupled to an anti-collapse auxiliary objective that maximizes the effective rank of the co-active atom set on each batch, preventing the importance weighting from collapsing several true features onto a single over-protected atom. The autoencoder is trained on residual-stream activations of a small transformer, and feature recovery is evaluated against known ground-truth features in the toy setting and by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on the model.
items.fabricated_claim: Replace the uniform L1 sparsity penalty in a sparse autoencoder with a per-atom penalty scaled by each feature's estimated importance (variance explained in downstream activations or measured effect on loss), so more important atoms receive a smaller penalty. Additionally subtract from each atom's penalty a term proportional to its mean squared cosine overlap with currently co-active atoms, and couple this to an auxiliary objective that maximizes the effective rank of the co-active atom set each batch to prevent collapse onto a single over-protected atom.

items.base: A generalization of the prefix-matching score used to identify induction heads that credits soft matches based on token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, rather than exact single-token identity. Heads scoring high on this generalized metric perform approximate copying and are treated as distinct from exact-match induction heads. Their causal contribution to few-shot in-context task accuracy is assessed by targeted ablation on inputs containing paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and their appearance is tracked relative to the in-context learning phase transition during training.
items.fabricated: A generalization of the prefix-matching score used to identify induction heads that credits soft matches based on token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, rather than exact single-token identity. Each soft-match credit is modulated by a semantic-coherence gate g_h = cos(k, q) / (1 + ||Δv||), the ratio of key–query cosine alignment to value-space displacement, which isolates heads that copy meaning rather than surface form along what we term the copy-suppression manifold. The soft-match acceptance threshold τ_h is set per head adaptively from that head's attention-entropy half-life, so heads with more diffuse attention are held to a stricter match. Heads scoring high on this gated metric perform approximate copying and are treated as distinct from exact-match induction heads. Their causal contribution to few-shot in-context task accuracy is assessed by targeted ablation on inputs containing paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and their appearance is tracked relative to the in-context learning phase transition during training.
items.fabricated_claim: Generalize the prefix-matching score used to identify induction heads so it credits approximate/soft matches based on token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context instead of exact single-token identity, classify heads that score high on this soft metric as approximate-copying heads distinct from exact-match induction heads, and test their causal contribution to few-shot in-context task accuracy by ablating them on inputs containing paraphrased or noisy repetitions.

items.base: Apply the standard corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol to a larger battery of factual relations and across several model sizes. Subject tokens are corrupted with Gaussian noise, and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to measure the indirect effect on the correct object's probability. Map how the peak mediating layer and token position vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, and report whether the mid-layer MLP localization observed for GPT-2 continues to hold across these expanded conditions.
items.fabricated: Apply the standard corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol to a larger battery of factual relations and across several model sizes. Subject tokens are corrupted with Gaussian noise whose covariance is aligned to the subject embedding's local tangent space and scaled per subject by an embedding-curvature term κ_s (the trace of the token-embedding Gram matrix over the subject span), so that corruption strength is matched in representational magnitude rather than raw activation magnitude across subjects of differing frequency. Clean activations are then restored at each layer and token position to measure the indirect effect on the correct object's probability, with each effect normalized by a per-relation mediation baseline estimated from the unrestored corrupted run. Map how the peak mediating layer and token position vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, and report whether the mid-layer MLP localization observed for GPT-2 continues to hold across these expanded conditions.
items.fabricated_claim: Run ROME-style corrupt-and-restore causal tracing across more relations and model sizes, but set each subject token's Gaussian corruption variance proportional to that token's embedding-norm percentile, and divide each layer/position restored indirect-effect score by the indirect effect obtained when restoring a non-subject filler token. Then map how the peak mediating layer and token position vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale.

items.base: Modify attention rollout by scaling each layer's attention matrix by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors before the multiplicative cross-layer aggregation, adding a magnitude term that the standard formulation omits. Evaluate the resulting token attributions on standard faithfulness benchmarks using deletion and insertion perturbation curves, and compare against gradient-times-input, across several pretrained transformer classifiers. Report whether the value-norm scaling shifts the perturbation curves relative to unweighted rollout.

items.base: Allocate a single fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads instead of a uniform per-head keep-size. Heads whose attention is concentrated on few positions receive smaller budgets; heads with dispersed attention receive larger budgets, holding the total constant. Within each head, the retained key/value set is still chosen exactly as in standard eviction, by accumulated attention score together with a recency window. Only the per-head split of the fixed budget is made adaptive.

items.base: An online controller sets the speculation length—the number of tokens drafted before each verification step—from a running estimate of the recent acceptance rate. The controller maintains an exponential moving average of accepted-token counts per step and chooses the next draft length to minimize expected wasted computation, drafting more tokens when recent acceptance has been high and fewer when it has been low. The draft and target models, the rejection-sampling acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee are all unchanged; only the per-step number of speculative tokens is set adaptively rather than being held fixed.

items.base: Before invoking the existing variable-length kernel, sort the sequences in each batch into length buckets and reorder the packed buffer accordingly, storing an inverse permutation to restore the original order after the kernel returns. Grouping similar-length sequences makes the work carried by concurrently launched query-blocks more uniform and collects ragged, partially-filled tail tiles together, reducing load imbalance across streaming multiprocessors. The attention kernel, its tiling, and its online-softmax computation are unchanged; only the host-side packing order and an index remapping are added, and outputs remain exact.

Bucket boundaries are not fixed but are placed by minimizing a tile-variance functional—the variance of predicted per-query-block FLOPs across concurrently launched blocks—whose minimizer, by a quantile-matched partitioning argument, sits at equal-mass quantiles of the batch's length distribution. Each bucket's occupancy is then rounded up to the nearest multiple of the streaming-multiprocessor count scaled by a hardware occupancy constant, a wavefront-alignment step that keeps every launched wave fully populated and eliminates the trailing partial wave. The attention kernel, its tiling, and its online-softmax computation are unchanged; only the host-side packing order and an index remapping are added, and outputs remain exact.

items.base: Define a width-indexed drift statistic equal to the relative Frobenius-norm change of the empirical neural tangent kernel between initialization and convergence. Measure this drift across a range of widths and standard architectures, fit its dependence on width, and test whether it decreases at an approximately inverse-width rate. Then relate the drift to the discrepancy between the test error predicted by kernel regression with the initial NTK and the actual test error of the trained finite-width network, assessing whether larger drift accompanies a larger prediction discrepancy.

items.base: Extend the directional-convergence analysis to gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses (e.g., the logistic loss). The normalized iterate still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) solution, with the momentum coefficient entering only the rate constants and lower-order terms, leaving the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) convergence order unchanged. Conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient are given under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges.

The analysis proceeds through a damped-margin potential—the normalized margin minus a friction term proportional to the velocity's projection onto the current parameter direction—which is shown to be monotone along the iterates and whose stationarity certifies directional convergence. The normalized iterate still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) solution, with the momentum coefficient beta entering only the rate constants and lower-order terms: the O(1/log t) leading constant is renormalized by the effective-friction factor 1/(1-beta), leaving the limiting direction and the convergence order unchanged. Convergence holds provided the momentum coefficient stays below a critical value beta_c = 1 - eta*L, where L is the loss's smoothness constant, above which the damped-margin potential loses monotonicity; conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient are given under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges.
items.fabricated_claim: Analyze gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on separable data under exponentially-tailed (e.g. logistic) losses using a 'damped-margin potential' (normalized margin minus a friction term proportional to velocity's projection on the parameter direction), showing directional convergence to the L2 max-margin/SVM solution with the O(1/log t) leading constant rescaled by 1/(1-beta). The load-bearing claim is that convergence/monotonicity holds exactly when momentum beta < beta_c = 1 - eta*L (eta = step size, L = loss smoothness constant), with the limiting direction and convergence order otherwise unchanged.

items.base: Reformulate the Chinchilla compute-optimal analysis under a total-lifetime compute budget that adds expected inference cost to training cost: C_total = C_train + C_inf, where inference compute grows with model size and with the number of tokens served by the deployed model. Using the fitted loss form L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β, minimize loss subject to the lifetime budget for a given expected query volume. Because inference cost scales with model size but not with training-token count, the constrained optimum shifts toward smaller models trained on more tokens as expected deployment volume increases. Report the corrected token-per-parameter ratio as a function of anticipated inference load.

Report this corrected token-per-parameter ratio as a function of anticipated inference load.

items.base: Apply the standard corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol to a larger battery of factual relations and across several model sizes. Subject tokens are corrupted with Gaussian noise, and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to measure the indirect effect on the correct object's probability. Map how the peak mediating layer and token position vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, and report whether the mid-layer MLP localization observed for GPT-2 continues to hold across these expanded conditions.

Second, clean activations are restored at each layer and token position, and the indirect effect on the correct object's probability is divided by a per-relation mediation baseline (the indirect effect obtained under a matched null restoration at a non-subject filler token) to yield a frequency-corrected mediation score. items.base: Extend PairNorm so that its single global rescaling constant is replaced by a rescaling factor that varies with node degree and with layer depth. Instead of holding one target for total pairwise distance across all nodes, the normalization target is set per degree bucket and per layer, with a small number of learned coefficients fit during training. The aggregation operator is otherwise unchanged. Because high-degree nodes smooth faster under Laplacian propagation, allowing their normalization scale to differ from low-degree nodes is intended to keep the anti-collapse effect better matched across the degree distribution as depth increases.

Concretely, each degree bucket b at layer l is assigned a target tau_{b,l} = tau_0 * (1 + beta * log(1 + d_b))^{-l/L}, a degree-conditioned decay rule whose exponent tracks the faster Dirichlet-energy contraction of high-degree nodes under Laplacian propagation. An auxiliary anti-collapse consistency loss penalizes the squared deviation between each layer's realized per-bucket pairwise distance and its target tau_{b,l}, and is added to the task loss with a fixed weight. items.base: Augment the input node features of a GIN with a fixed set of precomputed structural descriptors — for example, counts of small subgraphs such as triangles and short cycles, together with shortest-path distance encodings to a set of anchor nodes. Because these descriptors take different values on some node pairs that 1-WL colors identically, the augmented model can distinguish certain pairs of graphs that standard message passing cannot. The proposal is to specify which descriptor sets provably lift distinguishing power above 1-WL for named graph families, and to measure the resulting gains on standard graph-classification benchmarks.
items.fabricated: Augment the input node features of a GIN with a fixed set of precomputed structural descriptors — for example, counts of small subgraphs such as triangles and short cycles, together with shortest-path distance encodings to a set of anchor nodes. Because these descriptors take different values on some node pairs that 1-WL colors identically, the augmented model can distinguish certain pairs of graphs that standard message passing cannot. To control which descriptors actually contribute, each descriptor channel k passes through a cycle-basis gate gamma_k = sigmoid(a * ehat_k), where ehat_k is the descriptor's local discriminative entropy — the entropy of its value distribution within a node's k-hop neighborhood — so descriptors that are locally constant, and therefore add no separating power, are suppressed before concatenation. A spectral separation certificate, computed from the rank of the stacked descriptor-difference matrix over each named graph family, selects the minimal descriptor set that provably lifts distinguishing power above 1-WL. The proposal is to specify which descriptor sets provably lift distinguishing power above 1-WL for named graph families using this certificate, and to measure the resulting gains on standard graph-classification benchmarks.

items.base: Extend DP-SGD by replacing the single global clipping norm with per-layer clipping norms, each set proportional to a running online estimate of that layer's average per-example gradient norm. Gaussian noise is calibrated separately for each layer to its assigned clipping norm, and the per-layer mechanisms are composed under the moments accountant to report a total (ε, δ). The allocation redistributes a fixed noise budget toward layers with larger gradients, aiming to reduce clipping-induced utility loss while preserving the same overall privacy guarantee as standard DP-SGD at matched total noise.

items.base: Run LiRA against image classifiers trained across a sweep of a single regularizer's strength (e.g., weight-decay coefficient or dropout rate), holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed. For each setting, measure the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates alongside the train-test generalization gap, and fit the resulting dose-response relationship between regularization strength and attack success. Report the functional form and identify the strength at which marginal privacy gains diminish, providing calibrated guidance for choosing regularization hyperparameters under a target attack-success budget.
