The fit is anchored to a leakage-susceptibility index — the trace of the per-example loss Hessian averaged over training points, estimated cheaply with Hutchinson probes — which, under a local-quadratic approximation of the loss landscape, predicts the attack's true-positive rate as a saturating function of regularization strength and yields a closed-form estimate of the strength at which per-example curvature, and hence leakage, flattens. Report the functional form and identify the strength at which marginal privacy gains diminish, cross-checking the Hessian-trace prediction against the empirical curve, providing calibrated guidance for choosing regularization hyperparameters under a target attack-success budget.
items.fabricated_claim: Sweep a single regularizer's strength with architecture, data, and optimization budget fixed, run LiRA measuring TPR at fixed low FPR alongside the train-test gap, and fit the dose-response of attack success versus regularization strength. Anchor the fit to a 'leakage-susceptibility index' equal to the Hutchinson-estimated trace of the averaged per-example loss Hessian, which under a local-quadratic approximation predicts attack TPR as a saturating function of strength and gives a closed-form estimate of where leakage flattens.

items.base: Instantiate the X-learner with a single shared-trunk neural network. One representation feeds two outcome heads for the treated and control responses; the first-stage imputed individual effects are then regressed by two further heads, and the two effect estimates are combined using an estimated propensity score. An integral-probability-metric penalty balances the treated and control representations, and the whole model is trained end-to-end rather than in separate stages.
items.fabricated: Instantiate the X-learner with a single shared-trunk neural network. One representation feeds two outcome heads for the treated and control responses; the first-stage imputed individual effects are then regressed by two further heads, and the two effect estimates are combined using an estimated propensity score. Rather than the standard propensity weighting, the two effect estimates are merged by an overlap-adaptive gate alpha(x) = sigmoid(kappa * (ohat(x) - rho)), where ohat(x) is a locally estimated overlap score — the smoothed density ratio of treated to control representations at x — and rho a learned threshold, so that in regions of poor overlap the estimator leans on whichever arm is better supported. A Neyman-orthogonal residual head predicts the first-order bias-correction term and is subtracted from the combined estimate, which is intended to make the effect estimate insensitive to small errors in the nuisance outcome and propensity models. An integral-probability-metric penalty balances the treated and control representations, and an auxiliary overlap-consistency loss penalizes disagreement between the gate's overlap score and the propensity head's implied density ratio. The whole model is trained end-to-end rather than in separate stages.
items.fabricated_claim: Build the X-learner as one shared-trunk network (a shared representation feeding two treated/control outcome heads plus two imputed-effect heads) trained end-to-end, combining the two effect estimates with an overlap-adaptive gate alpha(x) = sigmoid(kappa*(ohat(x) - rho)) on a local smoothed treated/control density ratio instead of propensity weighting. Subtract a Neyman-orthogonal bias-correction head from the combined estimate, and add an IPM representation-balancing penalty plus an overlap-consistency loss between the gate's overlap score and the propensity head's implied density ratio.

items.base: Reduce the dependence of the double machine learning estimator on the particular random cross-fitting partition by computing the orthogonal-score estimate over many independent partitions and aggregating the point estimates with a median-of-means combiner. Adjust the variance estimator to add the between-partition variability to the usual within-partition sandwich term, so that confidence intervals account for fold-assignment uncertainty. Estimate the nuisance functions on each fold with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss. The estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic theory remain those of the original framework.
items.fabricated: Reduce the dependence of the double machine learning estimator on the particular random cross-fitting partition by computing the orthogonal-score estimate over many independent partitions and aggregating the point estimates with a median-of-means combiner. Before aggregation, each partition's estimate is reweighted by an orthogonality-defect gate: we compute d_k, the empirical inner product between the out-of-fold score residuals and the nuisance-estimation errors on partition k, and scale the k-th estimate by exp(-lambda d_k^2), so that partitions whose fold assignment leaks nuisance error into the score contribute less. The gate scale lambda is set adaptively to the value that minimizes a second-order Neyman-orthogonality curvature bound on the aggregated estimator, which cancels the leading partition-induced bias term. Adjust the variance estimator to add the between-partition variability to the usual within-partition sandwich term, together with a curvature correction that accounts for the defect-weighting, so that confidence intervals account for fold-assignment uncertainty. Estimate the nuisance functions on each fold with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss. The estimand and orthogonal moment remain those of the original framework.
items.fabricated_claim: Run the double machine learning orthogonal-score estimator over many random cross-fitting partitions and combine the per-partition estimates with a median-of-means combiner, but first downweight each partition by exp(-lambda*d_k^2), where d_k is the empirical inner product between that partition's out-of-fold score residuals and its nuisance-estimation errors and lambda is chosen to minimize a second-order orthogonality curvature bound. Inflate the variance estimate by adding between-partition variability (plus a weighting-curvature correction) to the usual within-partition sandwich term.

items.base: We replace the single channel-shared linear map of the DLinear baseline with several channel-grouped linear heads. Channels are partitioned by correlation-based clustering, and each group is given its own trend and seasonal weight matrices, while the moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output are kept. The method adds a clustering step and group-specific parameters relative to the shared-weight baseline. Evaluation is on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons.
items.fabricated: We replace the single channel-shared linear map of the DLinear baseline with several channel-grouped linear heads. Channels are partitioned by correlation-based clustering, and each group is given its own trend and seasonal weight matrices, while the moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output are kept. Each channel's forecast is formed by a spectral-coherence gate that convexly combines its group head with a shared global head; the mixing weight is the channel's normalized dominant seasonal-frequency energy passed through a learned logistic map, so channels whose spectrum is poorly explained by their group revert toward the shared head. Training adds an inter-group decorrelation objective that penalizes the Frobenius inner product between the weight matrices of different groups, motivated by a bias-variance decomposition over cluster granularity that identifies cross-group weight redundancy as the dominant excess-variance term. The method adds a clustering step, the coherence gate, and group-specific parameters relative to the shared-weight baseline. Evaluation is on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons.
items.fabricated_claim: Replace DLinear's single channel-shared linear map with per-group trend and seasonal linear heads, where channels are partitioned by correlation-based clustering, and form each channel's forecast as a convex blend of its group head and a shared global head with mixing weight equal to the channel's normalized dominant seasonal-frequency energy passed through a learned logistic. Add a training penalty on the Frobenius inner product between the weight matrices of different groups.

items.base: A Chronos-style tokenized forecaster is extended to condition on exogenous covariates. Each covariate channel is scaled and quantized with the same binning scheme used for the target series, producing covariate tokens that are interleaved with target tokens along the sequence, with channel-identifying information added to the embeddings. The backbone is fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series that carry aligned covariates, leaving the tokenizer and next-token objective unchanged. At inference, known future covariates are supplied as context and target values are decoded autoregressively as before. Evaluation compares zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets where side information is available.
items.fabricated: A Chronos-style tokenized forecaster is extended to condition on exogenous covariates. Each covariate channel is scaled and quantized, producing covariate tokens that are interleaved with target tokens along the sequence, with channel-identifying information added to the embeddings; the per-covariate bin resolution is set adaptively from an estimate of each covariate's mutual information with the target, so weakly informative channels receive a coarser codebook. A covariate-relevance gate multiplies the attention contribution of each covariate token by a learned predictive-utility score, and the backbone is fine-tuned with an auxiliary covariate-masking self-distillation objective that trains the covariate-conditioned prediction to agree with the covariate-free prediction in proportion to the gate's confidence, suppressing spurious reliance on covariates that carry no future signal. The tokenizer family and next-token objective are otherwise unchanged. At inference, known future covariates are supplied as context and target values are decoded autoregressively as before. Evaluation compares zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets where side information is available.
items.fabricated_claim: Extend a Chronos-style tokenized forecaster to exogenous covariates by scaling and quantizing each covariate channel into tokens interleaved with the target tokens (with per-covariate bin resolution set from the covariate's estimated mutual information with the target) and multiplying each covariate token's attention contribution by a learned predictive-utility score. Fine-tune with a covariate-masking self-distillation loss that makes the covariate-conditioned prediction agree with the covariate-free prediction in proportion to the gate's confidence.

items.base: First-order MAML and Reptile are extended with per-layer inner-loop learning rates that are meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer's parameters are updated using first-order task gradients scaled by that layer's learned step size, and the outer loop optimizes both the initialization and the vector of step sizes on post-adaptation task loss. The method retains the first-order compute and memory cost of FOMAML and Reptile, adding only a small set of scalar step-size parameters, and is evaluated on standard few-shot classification benchmarks against fixed-step baselines.
items.fabricated: First-order MAML and Reptile are extended with per-layer inner-loop learning rates that are meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer's parameters are updated using first-order task gradients scaled by that layer's learned step size, which is additionally modulated by a gradient-alignment gate: the effective step is multiplied by the cosine similarity between the current task gradient and a running exponential-average meta-gradient for that layer, so layers whose task gradient conflicts with the consolidated meta-direction adapt less. The outer loop optimizes the initialization, the vector of step sizes, and a curvature-consistency regularizer that keeps the learned step sizes proportional to the inverse diagonal of an online Gauss-Newton estimate, which makes the update a first-order approximation to the implicit-MAML preconditioner. The method retains the first-order compute and memory cost of FOMAML and Reptile, adding only a small set of scalar step-size parameters and the running meta-gradient statistics, and is evaluated on standard few-shot classification benchmarks against fixed-step baselines.
items.fabricated_claim: Add per-layer inner-loop learning rates to first-order MAML/Reptile, meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization, and scale each layer's adaptation step by the cosine similarity between the current task gradient and a running exponential-average meta-gradient for that layer. Regularize the step sizes to stay proportional to the inverse diagonal of an online Gauss-Newton curvature estimate.

items.base: Combine EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty with a small experience-replay buffer in a single training objective: at each step, train on current-task data plus a minibatch sampled from the buffer while retaining the EWC anchoring term on parameters important to past tasks. The buffer supplies the replay gradient and can also be used to re-estimate the Fisher importance weights online rather than only at task boundaries. The relative weighting of the replay loss and the regularization penalty is tuned per task, and the method is evaluated on standard task-incremental benchmarks against each component in isolation.
items.fabricated: Combine EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty with a small experience-replay buffer in a single training objective: at each step, train on current-task data plus a minibatch sampled from the buffer while retaining the EWC anchoring term on parameters important to past tasks. Each parameter's anchor is passed through a drift-aware gate that decays its Fisher weight by the alignment between the replay gradient and the EWC gradient on that parameter, on the principle that a parameter already protected by replay should not be doubly constrained; the gate is derived as the minimizer of an upper bound on the post-update loss increase on past tasks. Buffer sampling is weighted by a per-example forgetting-susceptibility score updated online from the running increase in each stored example's loss. The buffer also re-estimates the Fisher importance weights online rather than only at task boundaries, and a consolidation-consistency term keeps the online Fisher estimate from drifting faster than the replay gradient can compensate. The relative weighting of the replay loss and the regularization penalty is tuned per task, and the method is evaluated on standard task-incremental benchmarks against each component in isolation.
items.fabricated_claim: Train with EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty plus a small experience-replay buffer in one objective, decaying each parameter's Fisher anchor weight by the alignment between its replay gradient and its EWC gradient. Sample the buffer by online per-example forgetting-susceptibility scores and re-estimate the Fisher importance weights online rather than only at task boundaries.

items.base: Replace the exact fairness criteria with epsilon-relaxed versions that permit bounded violations of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance. Derive the feasible region of jointly achievable (epsilon_cal, epsilon_fpr, epsilon_fnr) tuples as a function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy, extending the exact impossibility to a quantitative tradeoff surface. Estimate this Pareto frontier on standard fairness benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, and report how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened, providing practitioners a budget-allocation view of the incompatibility.
items.fabricated: Replace the exact fairness criteria with epsilon-relaxed versions that permit bounded violations of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance, and derive the feasible region of jointly achievable (epsilon_cal, epsilon_fpr, epsilon_fnr) tuples as a function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy. The frontier is governed by a conserved quantity we term the impossibility invariant, I = epsilon_cal + kappa * (epsilon_fpr + epsilon_fnr), where the exchange coefficient kappa is fixed by a base-rate divergence identity that links the between-group base-rate difference to the Fisher information of the group-conditional score distribution; I stays constant along the Pareto frontier, so tightening one criterion forces a proportional loosening of the others at the rate kappa. A marginal-substitution regulator then allocates a fixed total violation budget across the three criteria by iteratively shifting slack toward whichever criterion has the flattest local frontier gradient, converging to the budget split that minimizes the maximum forced violation. Estimate this frontier and the fitted kappa on standard fairness benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, and report how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened, providing practitioners a budget-allocation view of the incompatibility.
items.fabricated_claim: Replace the three exact group-fairness criteria (within-group calibration, false-positive-rate balance, false-negative-rate balance) with epsilon-relaxed versions permitting bounded violations, and characterize the feasible region of jointly achievable (eps_cal, eps_fpr, eps_fnr) tuples via a conserved quantity I = eps_cal + kappa*(eps_fpr + eps_fnr) that stays constant along the Pareto frontier, where the exchange coefficient kappa is fixed by an identity linking the between-group base-rate difference to the Fisher information of the group-conditional score distribution. Allocate a fixed total violation budget with a regulator that iteratively shifts slack toward the criterion with the flattest local frontier gradient (minimizing the maximum forced violation), and fit kappa and the frontier on COMPAS and Adult.

items.base: Combine the two estimators by keeping dropout active at test time within each member of a deep ensemble, so the final predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks. Sweep the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against plain ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets. Report the calibration-versus-cost frontier to identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at reduced training cost.
items.fabricated: Combine the two estimators by keeping dropout active at test time within each member of a deep ensemble, so the final predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks. Route the two sources of stochasticity through a per-example epistemic gate g(x) that sets the effective dropout temperature from the ratio of within-member to across-member predictive variance; a closed-form derivation of the mixture's predictive variance shows this ratio equals the calibration-optimal mixing coefficient, so g(x) increases mask sampling on inputs where the members agree and defers to member averaging where they disagree. During training, add a mask-orthogonality penalty that pushes the dropout masks drawn by different members toward complementary subnetworks, decorrelating their errors and, through the same variance decomposition, tightening the epistemic component of the estimate. Sweep the number of members and the base dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against plain ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets, and report the calibration-versus-cost frontier to identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at reduced training cost.
items.fabricated_claim: Keep dropout active at test time within each member of a deep ensemble so the predictive distribution mixes over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks, and route the two stochasticity sources through a per-example gate g(x) that sets the dropout temperature from the ratio of within-member to across-member predictive variance, a ratio taken via a closed-form predictive-variance decomposition to equal the calibration-optimal mixing coefficient (so more mask sampling is used where members agree, member averaging where they disagree). Add a mask-orthogonality penalty pushing different members' dropout masks toward complementary subnetworks to decorrelate errors, and sweep member count and base dropout rate measuring ECE, NLL, and OOD detection against plain ensembles and MC-dropout at matched compute.

items.base: Within the inverse-propensity framework, replace the single global popularity-based propensity per item with propensities conditioned on user-side covariates such as activity level and coarse demographic group, fit with a regularized logistic model over item and user features. Combine the resulting weights with self-normalization and propensity clipping to control estimator variance, then reweight the implicit-feedback training loss as usual. Propensities are re-estimated per user segment, so highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal.
items.fabricated: Within the inverse-propensity framework, replace the single global popularity-based propensity per item with propensities conditioned on user-side covariates such as activity level and coarse demographic group, fit with a regularized logistic model over item and user features, then combine the resulting weights with self-normalization and propensity clipping to control estimator variance. Tie the per-segment logistic models through a shared latent exposure factor so that segment propensities are partially pooled toward a common estimate, which reduces the reweighted estimator's variance by a factor equal to the inverse effective number of segments. Add a second-order drift-correction term that subtracts the propensity model's own bias, estimated from its influence function on the reweighted loss, removing the residual bias that covariate misspecification would otherwise leave in the IPS estimate. The clipping threshold is set per segment by a closed-form rule tied to the segment's effective sample size, and propensities are re-estimated per user segment, so highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal.
items.fabricated_claim: In inverse-propensity-scoring for implicit-feedback recommendation, replace the single global item-popularity propensity with per-item propensities conditioned on user covariates (activity level, coarse demographic group) fit by a regularized logistic model, tie the per-segment logistic models through a shared latent exposure factor that partially pools segment propensities and reduces estimator variance by a factor equal to the inverse effective number of segments, and add a second-order drift-correction term that subtracts the propensity model's own bias estimated from its influence function on the reweighted loss. Stabilize with self-normalization and a per-segment clipping threshold set by a closed-form rule tied to the segment's effective sample size, re-estimating propensities per user segment.

items.base: A connector that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel and concatenates their outputs. The Q-Former contributes a fixed set of compressed query tokens and the projection contributes the full set of per-patch tokens; a learned scalar gate weights the two streams before they are prepended to the text. The module is trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, so it combines the compression of query-based bridging with the spatial coverage of direct projection.
items.fabricated: A connector that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel and concatenates their outputs, replacing the single learned scalar gate with a content-adaptive routing gate. The Q-Former contributes a fixed set of compressed query tokens and the projection contributes the full set of per-patch tokens; the gate reads a per-image visual-token entropy, defined as the dispersion of the encoder's patch-feature attention, and allocates both the mixing weight and the number of retained patch tokens accordingly, routing more capacity to the query stream for low-entropy, spatially redundant images and to the projection stream for high-entropy ones. A redundancy penalty, derived from an information-bottleneck bound on the content shared between the two streams, is applied during training to decorrelate the query and patch tokens so that each contributes non-overlapping information before they are prepended to the text. The module is trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, so it combines the compression of query-based bridging with the spatial coverage of direct projection.
items.fabricated_claim: Build a vision-language connector that runs a Q-Former (fixed set of compressed query tokens) and an MLP per-patch projection (full set of per-patch tokens) in parallel and concatenates their outputs, replacing the single learned scalar gate with a content-adaptive routing gate that reads a per-image visual-token entropy (dispersion of the encoder's patch-feature attention) and sets both the mixing weight and the number of retained patch tokens from it (more query capacity for low-entropy images, more projection capacity for high-entropy ones). Add a redundancy penalty derived from an information-bottleneck bound on the content shared between the two streams to decorrelate the query and patch tokens, and train on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen.

items.base: Within the DARTS supernet, parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a systematically different scale than parameterized operations, which biases the architecture gradients toward them. The proposal normalizes each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture is formed, so that all candidates contribute at comparable magnitude, and adds a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution to slow premature collapse. The change is evaluated by the resulting improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy.
items.fabricated: Within the DARTS supernet, parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a systematically different scale than parameterized operations, which biases the architecture gradients toward them. The proposal normalizes each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture is formed, and additionally rescales each operation's architecture gradient by an operation-conditioned gain coefficient, the estimated sensitivity of the supernet loss to that operation's output scale, so that gradient magnitude reflects preference rather than residual scale. A collapse-barrier term augments the small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution: because premature collapse toward skip connections corresponds to a saddle in the architecture landscape, the term follows a curvature-adaptive schedule that raises the entropy weight whenever the Hessian of the architecture loss develops a dominant negative eigenvalue, converting the saddle into a repeller. The change is evaluated by the resulting improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy.
items.fabricated_claim: In the DARTS supernet, normalize each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before forming the architecture-weighted mixture, and rescale each operation's architecture gradient by an operation-conditioned gain coefficient equal to the estimated sensitivity of the supernet loss to that operation's output scale. Augment the entropy penalty on the architecture distribution with a collapse-barrier term on a curvature-adaptive schedule that raises the entropy weight whenever the Hessian of the architecture loss develops a dominant negative eigenvalue (turning the skip-collapse saddle into a repeller), and evaluate by the improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy.

items.base: Extend GPTQ's Hessian-guided quantization by combining it with the outlier-channel protection from LLM.int8: identify the highest-magnitude activation channels from calibration statistics, keep the corresponding weight columns in higher precision, and run GPTQ's second-order error compensation over the remaining 4-bit columns. A per-layer threshold on calibration activation magnitude selects which channels to protect, and the protected fraction is tuned to trade off model size against accuracy. This yields a mixed-precision GPTQ variant where the error-compensation update accounts for the already-fixed high-precision columns.
items.fabricated: Extend GPTQ's Hessian-guided quantization by combining it with the outlier-channel protection from LLM.int8, but replace raw magnitude thresholding with a Hessian-coupled outlier saliency score. For each channel, saliency is defined as its calibration activation magnitude scaled by its Hessian leverage — the diagonal Hessian entry times the summed absolute off-diagonal coupling in that column — which upper-bounds the second-order error that quantizing the channel would leak into its neighbors under GPTQ's compensating update (the layerwise leakage bound). Channels above a per-layer saliency threshold keep their weight columns in higher precision; GPTQ's second-order error compensation then runs over the remaining 4-bit columns. Crucially, the compensation is augmented with a residual reinjection step: the error that the fixed high-precision columns would otherwise have absorbed is projected back onto the 4-bit columns through the inverse-Hessian coupling, so the protected fraction can be reduced without leaving that error uncorrected. The saliency threshold and protected fraction are tuned jointly to trade off model size against accuracy, yielding a mixed-precision GPTQ variant whose error-compensation update accounts for the already-fixed high-precision columns.
items.fabricated_claim: Quantize a layer with GPTQ but keep in higher precision the weight columns whose saliency exceeds a per-layer threshold, where saliency = calibration activation magnitude times Hessian leverage (the diagonal Hessian entry times the summed absolute off-diagonal coupling in that column). Quantize the remaining columns to 4-bit with GPTQ's second-order error compensation modified to account for the fixed high-precision columns, and add a residual-reinjection step that projects the error those fixed columns would have absorbed back onto the 4-bit columns through the inverse-Hessian coupling. The saliency threshold and protected fraction are tuned jointly.

items.base: At a fixed parameter budget, rank structural groups (convolutional channels and attention heads) by an importance score aggregated from movement-pruning statistics — the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning — and remove the lowest-scoring groups. Compare this against the same structured procedure using aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm) as the score. Both variants use iterative pruning with weight rewinding to the original initialization. Evaluate at matched sparsity levels, reporting task accuracy and measured inference latency across vision and language backbones.
items.fabricated: At a fixed parameter budget, rank structural groups (convolutional channels and attention heads) by a coherence-weighted movement score. The base importance is the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning (movement-pruning statistics), but this is modulated by a directional coherence factor: the mean pairwise cosine alignment of the per-weight movement vectors within the group over fine-tuning. The motivation is that a group whose weights move coherently in the same direction behaves as a single functional unit, so its movement magnitude reflects genuine task-relevant capacity rather than the incidental sum of independently drifting weights; incoherent groups have their scores down-weighted because their apparent importance is an aggregation artifact. Groups are pruned below a coherence-gated threshold that adapts per iteration to keep the retained set's mean coherence above a target, removing the lowest-scoring groups to meet the budget. Compare this against the same structured procedure using aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm) as the score. Both variants use iterative pruning with weight rewinding to the original initialization. Evaluate at matched sparsity levels, reporting task accuracy and measured inference latency across vision and language backbones.
items.fabricated_claim: Structurally prune convolutional channels and attention heads under a fixed budget by scoring each group with its movement-pruning importance (summed tendency of the group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning) multiplied by a directional-coherence factor (the mean pairwise cosine alignment of the per-weight movement vectors within the group), removing the lowest-scoring groups under a per-iteration adaptive threshold that keeps the retained set's mean coherence above a target, using iterative pruning with weight rewinding to the original initialization; compared against the same procedure scored by group L2 norm.

items.base: Under a fixed adapter-parameter budget, assign each LoRA layer a rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix (for example, its stable rank or spectral energy) computed once before training, instead of applying a single uniform rank to all adapted layers. Ranks are rounded to meet the budget, while the scaling factor and the rest of the LoRA parameterization are unchanged. The resulting allocation is compared against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts.
items.fabricated: Under a fixed adapter-parameter budget, assign each LoRA layer a rank in proportion to a curvature-adjusted stable rank of its pretrained weight matrix, computed once before training instead of applying a single uniform rank to all adapted layers. The base statistic is the layer's stable rank (squared Frobenius norm over squared spectral norm); it is then multiplied by a curvature-adjustment exponent estimated from a single calibration backward pass, defined as the ratio of the layer's gradient energy to its weight energy — a cheap proxy for how sensitive the loss is to updates at that layer. The justification is that spectral bulk sets how many directions a layer could use, while loss curvature sets how many it will actually benefit from moving, so allocating capacity by their product concentrates rank where updates are both expressible and impactful. A single tempering exponent applied to this product controls how sharply the budget is skewed toward high-curvature layers and is the only added hyperparameter. Ranks are rounded to meet the budget, while the scaling factor and the rest of the LoRA parameterization are unchanged. The resulting allocation is compared against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts.
items.fabricated_claim: Under a fixed adapter-parameter budget, assign each LoRA layer a rank proportional to its stable rank (squared Frobenius norm over squared spectral norm) times a curvature factor (ratio of the layer's gradient energy to its weight energy from a single calibration backward pass), with this product raised to one tempering exponent that controls how sharply the budget skews toward high-curvature layers; ranks are computed once before training and rounded to meet the budget, with the LoRA scaling and parameterization otherwise unchanged.

items.base: Extend forward loss correction by re-estimating the class-conditional noise transition matrix at fixed intervals during training rather than once from a warm-up model. At each re-estimation step, high-confidence anchor examples per class are identified from the current model, and the transition matrix is updated as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates. The corrected loss is then computed with this running matrix. The approach keeps the class-conditional noise assumption and the forward-correction objective, changing only the schedule of transition-matrix estimation and its stabilization across updates.
items.fabricated: Extend forward loss correction by re-estimating the class-conditional noise transition matrix at fixed intervals during training rather than once from a warm-up model. At each re-estimation step, high-confidence anchor examples per class are identified from the current model, but each anchor's contribution is weighted by an anchor-purity gate — the margin between its top-two predicted probabilities — so that near-boundary anchors, which are the ones most likely to already be memorized noise, contribute little to the estimate. The successive per-step estimates are combined by an exponential moving average whose rate is drift-adaptive: it is set from a transition drift statistic, the Frobenius distance between the current and previous estimate, so that the running matrix tracks quickly while the estimate is still moving and freezes as it stabilizes. This is motivated by treating label-noise fitting as a non-stationary process — as the network progressively memorizes noisy labels, the effective observed-label channel drifts — so a fixed averaging rate either lags the drift or fails to denoise, whereas coupling the rate to measured drift keeps the correction matched to the current memorization regime. The corrected loss is then computed with this running matrix. The approach keeps the class-conditional noise assumption and the forward-correction objective, changing the schedule of transition-matrix estimation, its drift-adaptive stabilization, and the purity-gated anchor selection.
items.fabricated_claim: In forward loss correction, re-estimate the class-conditional noise transition matrix at fixed intervals during training from the current model's per-class high-confidence anchors, weighting each anchor by its top-two predicted-probability margin (purity gate), and combine successive estimates with an exponential moving average whose rate is set from the Frobenius distance between the current and previous estimate (tracking fast while the estimate moves, freezing as it stabilizes); the corrected loss is computed with this running matrix.

items.base: The energy score is computed as a single scalar from the logits and compared against a global threshold. Because different classes exhibit systematically different energy magnitudes, the method estimates, for each class, the mean and standard deviation of the energy over in-distribution training inputs assigned to that class. At test time, an input's energy is standardized using the statistics of its predicted class, and the standardized value is thresholded for OOD detection. The correction requires only stored per-class statistics and no retraining or auxiliary outlier data.
items.fabricated: The energy score is computed as a single scalar from the logits and compared against a global threshold. Because different classes exhibit systematically different energy magnitudes, the method estimates, for each class, the mean and standard deviation of the energy over in-distribution training inputs assigned to that class. Rather than standardizing with these moments alone, it additionally fits a per-class tail index: within-class in-distribution energy is not Gaussian but follows a class-specific Gumbel-type tail (the log-sum-exp of near-Gaussian logits concentrates its right tail), so the standardized value is passed through a tail-index correction that reweights deviations by the estimated per-class shape parameter, making a fixed standardized threshold correspond to the same tail probability across classes. The standardized energy is also multiplied by a margin-decoupling factor — the per-class correlation between energy and the top-two logit margin, estimated on the same in-distribution inputs — which removes the component of energy that merely tracks softmax confidence and is therefore uninformative about density. At test time, an input's energy is standardized using the statistics of its predicted class, tail-corrected, margin-decoupled, and thresholded for OOD detection. The correction requires only stored per-class statistics (moments, tail index, and margin correlation) and no retraining or auxiliary outlier data.
items.fabricated_claim: For OOD detection, standardize an input's energy score using the per-class mean and standard deviation of energy over in-distribution training inputs of its predicted class, then apply a per-class tail-index correction that reweights the standardized deviation by a fitted class-specific Gumbel-type shape parameter so a fixed standardized threshold corresponds to the same tail probability across classes, and multiply by a margin-decoupling factor (the per-class correlation between energy and the top-two logit margin) to remove the confidence-tracking component; threshold the result, using only stored per-class statistics and no retraining.
